positions.technicalDetails:
SUBSTITUTE TEACHER · FAIRFAX COUNTY PUBLIC SCHOOLS (FCPS)
Fairfax County, Virginia · One of the 10 largest school districts in the United States

THE DISTRICT & THE STAGE
Fairfax County Public Schools is not just any school district. With over 180,000 students enrolled across more than 200 schools, it is one of the ten largest public school systems in the entire United States and consistently ranks as one of the highest-performing districts in the country. It serves one of the most ethnically, linguistically, and economically diverse communities in the nation — a region where more than 100 languages are spoken at home and students come from every background imaginable. Stepping into this district as a substitute teacher means stepping into that complexity every single day. It means being trusted to walk into any school, in any part of the county, and represent the standard of education that FCPS students and families expect and deserve.

This is the environment that shapes everything about what it means to be a substitute teacher here. It is not a quiet, predictable job. It is a front-line, people-first, think-on-your-feet role that demands every professional and personal skill you have — and then asks for a little more.

HOW THE DAY ACTUALLY BEGINS — THE LOGISTICS
The morning of a substitute teaching assignment starts well before the first bell rings. FCPS uses a digital scheduling and assignment system called SmartFindExpress — commonly referred to as SFE — to match available substitutes with open classroom positions across the district. When an assignment comes in, whether the night before or early that same morning, the substitute accepts the position and receives the school name, grade level, subject, and start time. From that moment forward, they are responsible for that classroom.

Arriving at the school, the first stop is always the main office. This is where the substitute checks in, signs in with the front desk staff, receives any visitor or staff badge, picks up the classroom key if needed, and is directed to the room. This check-in process is not just procedural — it is also the moment where the substitute becomes an official presence in that building for the day, accountable to the administration and subject to all the same professional conduct standards as a full-time employee. FCPS takes its substitute program seriously, and the expectation from the moment you walk through the door is that you carry yourself like a professional educator — because for that day, that is exactly what you are.

Once at the classroom, the work of preparation begins immediately. If the absent teacher has left sub notes — a written plan of what to teach, what to hand out, how the class is structured, and any important information about individual students — the substitute reads through everything carefully before students arrive. These notes can range from extremely detailed, with minute-by-minute instructions and pre-copied worksheets ready to go, to almost nothing at all. When there is no plan left, the substitute must quickly assess the classroom environment, look at any visible curriculum materials, textbooks, or posted schedules, and construct a reasonable approach for the day on the spot. This is not a rare situation — it happens regularly, and handling it without showing any disruption to the students is a skill that only comes with experience and composure.

THE CLASSROOM — RUNNING THE ROOM
When students walk in and see a substitute teacher standing at the front of the room, their reaction tells you everything about what kind of day you are about to have. Some classes are immediately well-behaved, curious, and ready to work. Others sense the change in routine and test it. Either way, the first five minutes of class are everything. The substitute must establish authority, presence, and warmth all at the same time — not by being intimidating, but by being calm, confident, and clear. Introducing yourself, setting expectations for behavior, and communicating the plan for the day in a way that students take seriously is a skill that looks simple from the outside but takes real emotional intelligence to execute well.

From there, the job of actually delivering instruction begins. In an elementary classroom, this might mean leading a phonics lesson with a group of second graders, guiding the class through a math activity using manipulatives, reading aloud from a class novel, facilitating a science experiment the teacher left instructions for, or simply keeping students on task as they complete independent work. The pace is constant. Young children need structure, redirection, encouragement, and attention in a way that is almost relentless — and the substitute has to provide all of it while also managing transitions between subjects, handling bathroom requests, taking attendance, monitoring behavior, and keeping an eye on the clock to make sure the schedule stays on track.

In a middle school classroom, the dynamic is completely different. Students at this age are in the middle of figuring out who they are, and they bring that energy — and that unpredictability — into every interaction. They are old enough to push back, smart enough to notice when an adult does not fully know the material, and social enough that peer dynamics in the room have a huge effect on how the class behaves. A substitute who walks in without confidence, without a plan, or without the ability to hold the room's attention will lose that class quickly. The ones who succeed here are the ones who show genuine interest, stay firm without being cold, and find ways to make the material relevant and engaging even when they are working off someone else's lesson plan.

In a high school classroom, the expectations shift again. Students are practically adults in many ways, and they respond best to being treated like it. The tone is less about managing energy and more about facilitating focused, mature work. High school classes often involve longer independent work periods, group discussions, test review sessions, or video-based lessons left by the teacher. The substitute's role here is part facilitator, part monitor, and part authority figure — making sure students are actually doing what they are supposed to be doing, answering questions when possible, and escalating to a department teacher or administrator when something comes up that is outside the scope of the sub notes.

TECHNICAL OPERATIONS — THE SYSTEMS, TOOLS & PROTOCOLS
Substitute teaching in a large public school district like FCPS is far more technically involved than most people realize. From the moment an assignment begins, the substitute is expected to navigate a range of school systems and tools accurately and professionally. Taking attendance is one of the most operationally critical tasks of the day — FCPS uses a digital attendance system, and substitutes are required to mark each student's status correctly and submit it within the first window of class. This is not just an administrative formality. Accurate attendance directly affects student records, safety accountability, and administrative tracking across the school.

Many FCPS classrooms are equipped with smartboards, projectors, document cameras, and integrated classroom audio systems. Substitutes are expected to operate this technology to deliver instruction — playing videos left by the teacher, pulling up digital worksheets, projecting reading passages, or using the board to walk through problems with students. The ability to troubleshoot a projector that will not connect, navigate an unfamiliar platform like Google Classroom or Canvas, or locate a saved assignment on the classroom computer — all while students are watching and waiting — is a real technical challenge that comes up regularly on the job.

Google Classroom is deeply embedded in how FCPS schools operate. Many teachers leave their day's assignments entirely within their digital classroom — assignments posted, resources linked, and submission dropboxes already set up. As a substitute, being able to walk students through where to find their work, how to submit it, and what to do when something is not loading correctly is an expected part of the role.

Beyond classroom technology, substitutes also operate within FCPS's broader school safety and communication systems. Every school has a specific protocol for emergencies — fire drills, lockdowns, shelter-in-place procedures — and substitutes are expected to know and follow these procedures exactly. During a drill or an actual emergency, the substitute is fully responsible for the students in their care.

SPECIAL POPULATIONS — SERVING EVERY STUDENT
One of the most meaningful and technically complex aspects of substitute teaching in FCPS is working across its specialized student populations. FCPS serves tens of thousands of students with Individualized Education Programs — IEPs — which are legally binding documents that outline the specific accommodations, modifications, and support structures each student is entitled to receive, every single day, regardless of whether their regular teacher is present. When a substitute is placed in a special education classroom, they are stepping into an environment that requires an entirely different skill set.

Working in an English as a Second Language classroom adds another layer of complexity. FCPS serves a massive immigrant and refugee population, and in ESL settings, the substitute is often working with students who are still building their English proficiency from the ground up. Communication requires patience, creativity, and a willingness to find ways to connect that go beyond spoken language.

Advanced academic classrooms — including FCPS's highly regarded AAP program — present a different kind of challenge. Students in these programs are working at an accelerated level and expect a substitute who can at least keep up with the material and manage a class of high-achieving, intellectually curious, and sometimes strong-willed students.

MANAGING PEOPLE — STUDENTS, STAFF & SITUATIONS
Every person who has ever worked in a school will tell you the same thing: the hardest part of the job is never the content. It is the people. And as a substitute, you are managing people — students, staff, and situations — in an environment where you are always the new face in the room. De-escalating conflict in the classroom is a regular part of the job. Knowing which response fits which situation, and executing it without escalating the room, is one of the most important and least talked-about skills in substitute teaching.

END OF DAY — CHECKOUT, NOTES & ACCOUNTABILITY
When the final bell rings and students leave the room, the substitute teacher's day is not quite finished. Before leaving the building, they are required to complete a formal checkout process and leave a detailed written summary for the returning teacher covering what was taught, how far the class got through the lesson plan, whether there were any behavioral issues, which students were absent, and anything else the teacher needs to know.

WHAT IT ALL ADDS UP TO
The skills built through this experience — adaptability, communication, leadership, emotional intelligence, cultural competency, technical proficiency, and the ability to perform under pressure with limited information — are not soft skills. They are real, transferable, high-value professional skills that apply in every field, every industry, and every leadership role. Substitute teaching at FCPS is not a stepping stone job. It is a proving ground — and the people who do it well are exactly the kind of people any organization should want on their team.

positions.technicalDetails:
There is something uniquely grounding about working in the lumber department at Home Depot. The department smells like raw wood and sawdust, the racks stretch high above your head stacked with thousands of boards, and the people who walk through those doors aren't browsing — they're building. They come in with a project on their mind, a deadline in their head, and sometimes a hand-drawn sketch in their pocket. My job was to meet them exactly where they were and make sure they left with exactly what they needed to bring that project to life.
As a Sales Specialist in the lumber and building materials department, I became the person customers turned to when the project got complicated. Someone would walk in needing wood for a deck they'd never built before, or a contractor would roll up needing a specific grade of lumber in a specific quantity, and I was the one who bridged the gap between what they knew and what they needed to know. I asked the right questions — What's the project? What's the load? Is this going indoors or outdoors? Do you need pressure-treated? — and from those answers, I helped them build a complete materials list, not just grab a few boards off the shelf. I made sure customers left the store with confidence, not confusion.
One of the most hands-on parts of my role was operating the power saw to cut lumber to custom sizes for customers on the spot. This wasn't something I took lightly. Every cut had to be accurate — a quarter inch off could mean a board that didn't fit, a project that stalled, and a customer who had to come back. I measured carefully, communicated clearly, and made sure every custom cut matched exactly what the customer needed for their specific application. There's a real satisfaction in handing someone a perfectly cut piece of wood and watching them realize the project just got a whole lot easier.
Beyond the cutting station, I was constantly moving. Helping customers load lumber, wood panels, and heavy building materials into their trucks and vehicles was a regular part of my day — and it wasn't just about muscle. It was about making sure every load was stacked safely, secured properly, and wouldn't shift during transport. A load of plywood that slides on the highway isn't just a lost product — it's a safety hazard. I took that responsibility seriously every single time.
The physical demands of the role extended to freight and inventory as well. I received shipments, unloaded deliveries, and moved product from the back of the store to the sales floor — keeping the department stocked, organized, and ready for the next customer who walked in. I operated electric pallet jacks and forklifts to move heavy loads of lumber and building materials safely throughout the store and yard, always following Home Depot's safety protocols and wearing the required personal protective equipment without exception. In an environment where the products weigh hundreds of pounds and the equipment is industrial, safety isn't a checkbox — it's a culture, and I was fully bought into it.
The product knowledge I built during my time in this role became one of my most valuable assets. I learned lumber from the inside out — wood species, grades, dimensional sizing, pressure treatment levels, moisture content, and the specific use cases that made one board right for a project and another completely wrong. I understood the difference between a #1 and #2 grade pine, knew when to steer a customer toward cedar versus treated lumber for an outdoor application, and could explain the structural difference between OSB and plywood in plain language that made sense to a first-time builder. That knowledge made my conversations with customers more than just transactional — they became genuinely helpful, which is what drives sales and earns loyalty.
The Yard also taught me how to function as part of a larger team. The lumber department doesn't operate in isolation — it connects to the rest of the store, to the loading dock, to receiving, to the pro desk, and to departments across the floor. I worked closely with teammates across multiple areas, covered other departments when the team needed support, and communicated constantly to keep the store running smoothly during high-traffic periods. When a big delivery came in and the whole team needed to mobilize, I showed up and pulled my weight. That kind of dependability — being someone your team can count on — mattered to me.
At the end of every shift, my section was stocked, straight, clean, and ready. Racks were organized, materials were safely stored, and the department looked the way a department should look when a customer walks in and needs to find something fast. That standard wasn't something I was told to maintain — it was something I held myself to, because I understood that a clean, well-organized department is itself a form of customer service.
What Home Depot gave me was a complete picture of what it means to be a true sales specialist in a high-volume retail environment — not just someone who rings up a transaction, but someone who understands the product deeply, serves the customer completely, operates equipment safely, and keeps the operation moving at full speed every single day.

positions.technicalDetails:
At Yard House, I stepped into one of the most demanding and rewarding roles in the hospitality industry — serving guests at one of the most recognized full-service restaurant and bar chains in the country. Yard House isn't just any restaurant. It's an experience. With a menu boasting over 100 beers on tap alongside an expansive, diverse food menu, every single shift required me to show up as not just a server, but as a guide, a host, and a brand ambassador all at once.
From the moment a guest walked through the door and sat down at one of my tables, I owned their entire experience. I greeted them warmly, made them feel at home, read the energy of the table, and set the tone for what I wanted to be a memorable visit. I took their food and drink orders with precision, stayed locked in on every detail — dietary restrictions, allergies, special requests — and made sure that information traveled accurately from my notepad to the kitchen and bar staff without anything getting lost in translation. When a dish had to come out exactly right because someone's health depended on it, I made sure it did.
One of the things I am most proud of from my time at Yard House is the deep product knowledge I built. I didn't just memorize a menu — I internalized it. I learned the stories behind craft beers, understood flavor profiles, knew which hops gave a beer its bitterness and which malts gave it its body. When a guest sat down feeling overwhelmed by 100+ taps staring back at them from the menu, I became their compass. I asked the right questions — Do you usually go for something light and crisp, or do you like something darker and fuller-bodied? Have you ever tried a sour? What about a hazy IPA? — and from their answers, I guided them toward something they didn't just like, but something they genuinely loved. I helped people discover new favorite beers they'd never have found on their own. That, to me, was one of the most satisfying parts of the job.
The Yard House environment is fast. During lunch and dinner rushes, the dining room is electric — tables turning, tickets flying, every team member moving with purpose. I learned how to manage multiple tables simultaneously without letting the quality of my attention drop at any one of them. I developed an internal system — a mental map of where every table was in their experience at any given moment — so that no guest ever felt forgotten, overlooked, or rushed. I stayed calm when the pressure was highest, and I leaned into the chaos rather than away from it.
I was also deeply embedded in the team that made the restaurant function. I didn't exist in a bubble. I communicated constantly with bussers, bartenders, and kitchen staff — calling out what was coming, flagging changes, making sure the floor stayed coordinated even when the volume was relentless. I reset tables quickly and cleanly between guests, keeping my section looking sharp and ready so that the next guest walking in saw the same polished experience as the one before them. When everyone played their role and communication was tight, the whole machine hummed — and I was proud to be a reliable part of that machine.
At the end of every visit, I closed out each table accurately — processing cash and card payments, managing open tabs, making sure every transaction was handled correctly. It was the final moment of the guest's experience, and I treated it with the same care I gave to the first.
What Yard House gave me was a masterclass in hospitality under pressure. I came out of it with the ability to command a section, command a conversation, and deliver a standard of service that made people want to come back.

positions.description:
This project addressed the unique challenges of penetrating the highly regulated electronic vaporizer market, where traditional advertising channels (TV, social media ads) are legally restricted. The primary objective was to engineer a high-traffic, compliant e-commerce platform capable of generating organic growth without reliance on paid media. The core challenge involved identifying market inefficiencies—specifically high-volume, low-difficulty keywords—and constructing a digital infrastructure that bridged logistical supply chains with aggressive Search Engine Optimization (SEO) requirements.

Leveraging a tech stack comprising Shopify, HTML, and JavaScript, I led the full-cycle development of a custom-optimized storefront. This involved designing high-fidelity UI/UX prototypes in Figma to enhance user engagement and coding responsive, fast-loading product pages to minimize bounce rates. I conducted extensive market research using Ahrefs to execute a precision SEO strategy, optimizing technical elements such as URL structures and content hierarchy. Additionally, I managed the backend business logic, integrating QuickBooks for financial oversight, establishing inventory tracking systems, and ensuring strict adherence to evolving legal and tax regulations.

The platform achieved remarkable success, scaling to approximately 100,000 monthly visitors and generating an average of $1,000 in daily revenue. Through the implementation of strategic conversion rate optimization (CRO) techniques, including automated email marketing funnels (Klaviyo) and dynamic upselling algorithms, the project successfully converted organic traffic into sustained sales. This initiative demonstrated the power of combining technical web development with data-driven market analysis to build a profitable, scalable online business in a competitive sector.

projects.title: Flashconverter: Financial Intelligence Platform
projects.description:
Built comprehensive financial intelligence platform to master personal finances for future homeownership, guided by the principle 'Money Saved is Money Made.' 

Engineered full-stack solution that transforms bank statement PDFs into actionable insights through automated Excel conversion and ML-powered transaction categorization achieving 70-95% confidence scores. Implemented intelligent learning system using IndexedDB that recognizes spending patterns over time, reducing manual categorization work by 80%.

Architected 100% client-side processing for complete financial privacy—zero server storage, zero data sharing. Built interactive dashboard with Chart.js visualizations tracking income, expenses, savings rate, and recurring payments across 19 smart expense categories.

Developed AI-powered savings optimizer delivering personalized recommendations, recurring subscription detection, and professional multi-sheet Excel exports with pre-formatted charts. Platform actively drives financial decision-making for personal wealth building while powering financial operations for consulting business.
projects.technicalDetails:
• **Architecture:** 100% Client-side application using Vanilla JavaScript (ES6 Modules), HTML5, and CSS3. Designed for privacy with no server-side file storage.
• **Data Pipeline:** Utilizes ConvertAPI for initial PDF-to-XLSX conversion via Base64. Data is then processed using SheetJS (parsing) and ExcelJS (cleaning, formatting, styling).
• **ML & Categorization:** features a custom local machine learning algorithm utilizing string similarity matching for merchant names. It calculates confidence scores (70%-95%) and improves accuracy over time by storing user-verified patterns in IndexedDB.
• **State Management & Storage:** leverage IndexedDB for persistent storage of transaction patterns and file history (~50MB+), LocalStorage for settings, and SessionStorage for temporary file transfer.
• **Auth & Viz:** InstantDB handles user authentication; Chart.js powers the data visualization (Income vs. Expenses, Category Breakdown).
• **Performance:** Average conversion speed of ~3 seconds with 99.9% extraction accuracy.

projects.title: Multi-Cloud Architecture: MIS 341 Final Project
projects.description:
In the modern landscape of enterprise IT, establishing secure, scalable, and interconnected cloud environments is a critical challenge. Organizations require robust mechanisms to bridge on-premise networks with cloud resources while maintaining strict security postures and reliable data storage strategies. This project addressed these needs by designing and deploying a comprehensive multi-cloud infrastructure leveraging both Amazon Web Services (AWS) and Microsoft Azure. The primary objective was to engineer a functional hybrid network, implement defense-in-depth security layers, and provision high-availability object storage systems across distinct cloud providers.

The solution involved a multi-faceted technical approach. On the networking front, I architected an AWS Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) environment, establishing a secure Site-to-Site VPN connection using Customer Gateways (CGW) and Virtual Private Gateways (VGW) configured with IKEv1 protocols to simulate enterprise hybrid connectivity. To secure compute resources, I implemented a layered security model for EC2 instances, utilizing both stateless Network Access Control Lists (NACLs) for subnet-level traffic filtering and stateful Security Groups for granular instance-level access control (specifically targeting TCP protocols 3389 for RDP and 22 for SSH). Furthermore, the project expanded into storage architecture by deploying AWS S3 buckets with custom JSON-based access policies to manage public/private object visibility, alongside Azure Storage Accounts where I configured Blob containers and managed file versioning via snapshots.

The result was a fully operational, secure cloud ecosystem that demonstrated seamless interoperability between network components and storage solutions. By successfully troubleshooting access denials through policy adjustments and verifying network traffic flows via specific route table configurations, the project validated the integrity of the security architecture. This implementation showcased a deep proficiency in cloud resource orchestration, affirming the ability to manage complex identity and access management (IAM) roles, network routing, and multi-cloud data lifecycles effectively.
key_features: **Hybrid Network Connectivity:** Engineered a Site-to-Site VPN tunnel utilizing AWS Virtual Private Gateways and Customer Gateways with IKEv1 configuration.
**Layered Security Architecture:** Implemented Defense-in-Depth strategies using stateless NACLs for subnet filtering and stateful Security Groups for EC2 instance protection.
**Multi-Cloud Storage Solutions:** Provisioned and managed object storage across AWS S3 and Azure Blob Storage, including bucket policy creation and snapshot versioning.
**Access Control Management:** Configured granular permissions and JSON-based resource policies to resolve access errors and enforce 'Least Privilege' principles.
**Protocol Optimization:** Configured specific TCP routing for remote administration via RDP (3389) and SSH (22) while maintaining strict inbound/outbound traffic rules.

projects.title: Retail Strategy: MIS 431 Final Project
projects.description:
In response to stagnant in-store revenue growth at PrimeStop Convenience, this project functioned as a strategic consultancy engagement designed to analyze customer behavior across the Mid-Atlantic region. The core business challenge was to identify the specific factors that drive the conversion of low-margin 'fuel-only' customers into higher-margin 'convenience shoppers.' Leveraging a dataset comprising customer demographics, transactional history, and store amenities, the objective was to replace intuition with rigorous predictive modeling to optimize marketing spend and operational strategy.

The technical methodology was executed using R and the `tidymodels` framework. The workflow initiated with a comprehensive Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) to evaluate variables such as income levels, store formats, and visit frequency. Subsequently, a robust machine learning pipeline was engineered, featuring data splitting, stratified sampling, and feature engineering recipes (including Yeo-Johnson transformations and dummy variable encoding). The project trained and evaluated three distinct classification algorithms—Logistic Regression, Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), and a hyperparameter-tuned Random Forest—alongside a linear regression model designed to forecast downstream visit volume for converted users.

The analysis identified the tuned Random Forest as the superior model based on F1-score performance. Variable Importance Plots (VIP) revealed that loyalty program enrollment and fuel visit frequency were the most significant predictors of conversion, validating early EDA hypotheses. These technical insights were synthesized into an executive-level report with actionable recommendations, such as restructuring the loyalty acquisition funnel and targeting high-frequency fuel visitors with cross-sell incentives. The project demonstrated the ability to translate complex data science workflows into tangible business strategies that directly address revenue goals.
key_features: Comparative analysis of three classification models (Logistic Regression, LDA, and Tuned Random Forest) to predict customer conversion.
Implementation of a rigorous machine learning workflow including 5-fold cross-validation and hyperparameter tuning.
Development of a linear regression model to forecast 'Total Convenience Visits' for engaged customers.
Executive-style business reporting that translates technical metrics (RMSE, F1 Score, AUC-ROC) into strategic action items.
Advanced feature engineering using the `recipes` package, applying normalization and zero-variance removal.

projects.title: Strategy Development: BUS 498 Capstone Project
projects.description:
Developed for the George Mason University Business Capstone, this strategic consulting report addresses the critical global challenge of ensuring food security for a population projected to grow by nearly 50% by 2050, despite shrinking arable land. Recognizing the saturation and cyclical nature of traditional agricultural machinery markets, the project analyzes the feasibility of Deere & Company diversifying into the high-growth Smart Urban Agriculture sector. The analysis identifies a gap in the market where Deere’s proprietary precision technology could solve inefficiencies in vertical farming operations.

The solution proposes the creation of a new division, "Deere Urban AgTech," designed to integrate AI, IoT robotics, and data analytics into modular indoor farming systems. The methodology utilized a rigorous suite of strategic management frameworks: PESTEL analysis to evaluate external macro-economic drivers, Porter’s Five Forces to assess industry attractiveness, and VRIO analysis to confirm Deere's internal competitive advantages in software and automation. The strategy pivots the business model from purely hardware sales to a "Hardware-as-a-Service" (HaaS) and subscription-based analytics model via the FarmOS platform.

The resulting feasibility study presents a comprehensive three-phase execution roadmap—from pilot capability building to global integration. Financial projections indicate that this strategic diversification would generate $300–$350 million in annual revenue by year three, expand operating margins from 14% to 17%, and reduce cyclical revenue dependency. Furthermore, the initiative aligns with ESG goals by targeting a 20% reduction in CO2 emissions, positioning Deere & Company not just as a machinery manufacturer, but as a dominant technology provider for sustainable urban food production.
key_features: **New Venture Formulation:** Detailed proposal for 'Deere Urban AgTech,' a new division leveraging existing R&D to capture the $30B smart-farming market.
**Analytical Rigor:** Applied advanced strategic frameworks (VRIO, PESTEL, Porter's Five Forces) to validate market entry and competitive advantage.
**Economic Logic & Modeling:** Projected a 15% ROI increase and margin expansion through recurring digital revenue streams and subscription analytics.
**Implementation Roadmap:** Designed a phased 5-year execution strategy covering pilot programs, commercial scale-up, and global integration.
**Sustainability Integration:** Aligned business objectives with environmental goals, targeting a 20% reduction in carbon footprint via renewable energy microgrids.

This paper recommends that John Deere expand into a new kind of farming: indoor and vertical farming. The main idea is that Deere should create a new division called Deere Urban AgTech that builds high-tech indoor farming systems using automation, sensors, and software. In simple terms, the paper says Deere is already strong in farming and technology, so it should use those strengths to become a leader in “farming inside buildings,” not only farming outdoors. 


The reason this strategy matters is because the world is changing fast. The paper explains that global food demand is expected to rise a lot in the future, while farmland and resources are becoming more limited. At the same time, more people are living in cities, and cities want reliable, local food supplies that do not depend on weather. Because of these trends, indoor and vertical farming are growing, and the paper argues this is a good moment for Deere to move into that space. 

The paper uses a PESTEL analysis to explain how the outside world supports this move. It says governments and policies are pushing sustainability and climate-friendly solutions, and many countries support smarter agriculture and clean technology. Economically, food demand and smart-farming markets are growing, which creates a big opportunity. Socially, people want cleaner, locally produced food and cities keep expanding. Technologically, tools like AI, sensors, and automation are improving quickly, which makes indoor farming more practical. Environmentally, the pressure on land and water makes indoor farming more attractive, and legally, stricter rules around the environment push companies to innovate in sustainable directions. 

The paper also uses Porter’s Five Forces to show what competition looks like and whether the industry can be profitable. It explains that the industry is not easy to enter because it takes money, expertise, and advanced technology to build indoor farming systems at scale. Competition exists because other agriculture and technology companies are also moving into smart farming. Buyers can have power because large customers care about costs and performance, and suppliers can have power because specialized components matter. Even with competition, the paper suggests Deere can compete well if it offers a complete solution that combines equipment, sensors, and software into one strong package. 

After explaining the outside world, the paper looks at Deere itself to answer, “Can Deere actually do this?” It describes Deere as more than a traditional equipment company, because Deere already works with data-driven tools, precision agriculture, monitoring, and advanced systems that help farmers improve performance. The paper argues that the same mix of hardware and technology can transfer into indoor farming, where machines, sensors, and software must work together every day. 

The paper also points out gaps Deere must take seriously. Because indoor farming depends heavily on connected technology, cybersecurity becomes a bigger risk, and the paper says Deere needs strong protection to avoid disruptions or data problems. It also mentions that Deere may need more specialized technology talent to build and scale this new division, because indoor farming systems require deep knowledge across hardware, software, data, and automated operations. 

To explain why Deere could have an advantage, the paper uses the VRIO framework. In simple words, VRIO asks if Deere’s strengths are valuable, uncommon, hard for others to copy, and supported by the company’s structure. The paper argues Deere’s combination of industry experience, engineering, technology investment, and long-term innovation makes it hard for many competitors to match quickly. It suggests Deere is organized in a way that can support large projects and continuous improvement, which is important if Deere wants to lead in a fast-growing market. 

The strategy itself is explained as building a new business unit that creates modular indoor farming systems using approaches like hydroponics and aeroponics, supported by automation, sensors, and analytics. The paper describes a platform idea (FarmOS) that would manage operations, collect data, and help optimize growing conditions. In simple terms, Deere would not just sell equipment; it would sell a complete indoor farming system that includes machines plus software plus ongoing support, so customers can run indoor farms more efficiently and predictably. 

The paper also explains who Deere should sell to and where it should focus first. It lists customers like urban farming companies, city partners, universities, and organizations focused on sustainability and food reliability. It also suggests starting in regions with strong infrastructure and strong interest in smart-city development—such as North America, Western Europe, and parts of East Asia—because those areas are more ready to adopt advanced indoor farming solutions. 

To show how Deere would win, the paper focuses on value creation through integration and learning. It explains that Deere can connect hardware and software in a way that creates better results over time. As more customers use the system, Deere can gather more data and improve its algorithms, which makes the system smarter and more useful. The paper also mentions partnerships with related industries (like lighting or other indoor farming components) and suggests that scale can reduce costs while improving performance, which strengthens Deere’s position. 

The paper includes trade-offs, meaning it explains what Deere should avoid to stay focused. It suggests Deere should not chase cheap, low-end markets that could weaken the brand. It also suggests Deere should not become a food seller itself, because the goal is to provide the systems and services that enable farming, not to compete directly as a food retailer. The paper emphasizes protecting Deere’s premium reputation while building a strong, professional product and service offering. 

Another important point is synergy, which means how this new plan fits with Deere’s existing business. The paper explains that Deere can use current strengths such as financing options (like leasing and subscriptions), existing service and maintenance capabilities, and technology teams that already understand agriculture data. In simple terms, the new division would not be separate from Deere; it would connect to what Deere already does well and create more stable, recurring revenue over time. 

On the money side, the paper argues the plan can be financially attractive because it adds recurring subscription revenue, reduces reliance on traditional equipment cycles, and creates long-term customer relationships through software and services. The paper provides estimates for future revenue and suggests margins could improve as the new business grows and becomes more efficient. It also discusses several ways Deere can create value, such as system sales, subscription analytics, predictive maintenance savings, and efficiency improvements. 

For execution, the paper lays out a phased rollout plan. First, Deere should run pilots and build capabilities through partnerships and testing, while tracking results like yields and energy efficiency. Second, Deere should scale production and launch commercially, bundling systems with subscriptions and financing to drive adoption. Third, Deere should expand globally and improve integration with sustainability goals, including tracking environmental performance and optimizing operations as the system matures. 

Finally, the paper explains the biggest risks and how to reduce them. It highlights cybersecurity risk because the systems are connected and could be targeted or disrupted, and it suggests partnering with experts and maintaining strong protections. It mentions zoning and regulations that may affect indoor farming projects, and it suggests working closely with cities and aligning with sustainability incentives. It also mentions talent challenges and recommends building training programs and hiring the right people. The conclusion returns to the main message: Deere should launch Deere Urban AgTech, start with pilots, manage the risks, and scale if the results prove strong.

projects.title: Autonomous AI Career Intelligence Platform
projects.description:
Modern job applications are broken for students and recent graduates—hours are spent tailoring resumes, writing cover letters, and hoping an ATS filter does not screen you out before a human ever reads your application. This platform solves that problem end-to-end with a fully autonomous, AI-powered career management system. By pasting a job description and pressing one button, the system researches the target company, analyzes the role requirements, rewrites and scores the resume, refines any detected gaps, and compiles a professionally formatted, ATS-optimized PDF resume alongside a personalized cover letter—entirely without manual intervention.

The platform's centerpiece is a six-step sequential AI pipeline powered by Anthropic Claude, which performs company intelligence research, structured job description extraction, content tailoring with hard budget constraints, a coherence review pass for cross-entry story consistency, a compatibility verdict with a 0–100 score, and targeted surgical refinement before generating LaTeX source code that XeLaTeX compiles into a polished PDF. Beyond resume generation, the system includes a streaming real-time chat assistant with multi-turn conversation history and vision support, a job application tracker with stored compatibility scores and application statuses, a skill gap analyzer delivering brutally honest assessments, an interview prep coach generating STAR-format answers grounded in the candidate's real experience, a coursework analyzer converting university PDFs and images into portfolio entries, an AI-curated tech news feed, a structured skill-builder with streak tracking, and a fully custom admin content management system.

All 18 API endpoints, four singleton services, the centralized AI client with exponential backoff retry logic, and the LaTeX compilation pipeline were engineered from scratch with no templates or starter kits. The result is a production-grade, full-stack application demonstrating end-to-end AI product design, document engineering, real-time streaming UX, and robust distributed error handling.

**Built by Jawad Iskandar**

## 1. The Story — Why I Built This

Job applications are broken for students and recent graduates. You spend hours tailoring a resume for one company, then another hour writing a cover letter, and by the time you apply you are not even sure if your resume will make it past the ATS (Applicant Tracking System) filter that automatically screens out resumes before a human ever sees them.

I decided to solve this problem for myself by building a system that does the entire job application process automatically and intelligently. The goal was clear: paste in a job description, press one button, and get back a professionally formatted, ATS-optimized, job-specific PDF resume and a personalized cover letter — both grounded in my real experience and tailored to the exact language and values of the company I am applying to.

That goal grew into this platform. What started as a resume tool became a full AI-powered portfolio and career management system. Along the way I built a streaming chat assistant, a job tracker, a skill gap analyzer, an interview prep coach, a coursework analyzer, and an AI-curated news feed — each one solving a specific problem in the job search process.

Every line of code in this system was written by me. There are no templates, no starter kits, no pre-built admin panels. This is an original, full-stack application engineered from scratch.

---

## 2. What The Platform Does

The platform has nine distinct AI-powered features and one content management system:

| Feature | What It Does |
|---|---|
| **Resume Tailoring Engine** | Autonomous 6-step AI pipeline that researches the company, analyzes the JD, rewrites your resume, scores it, refines gaps, and compiles a PDF |
| **Cover Letter Generator** | Generates a complete, personalized cover letter grounded in company research and tailored resume content |
| **Streaming Chat Assistant** | Real-time AI chatbot that knows your entire portfolio and can navigate the site on command |
| **Job Tracker** | Save, track, and manage job applications with compatibility scores and application status |
| **Skill Gap Analyzer** | Compares your portfolio against any JD and gives a brutally honest 0–100 score with a specific improvement plan |
| **Interview Prep Coach** | Generates STAR-format answers to application and interview questions tailored to the specific job and company |
| **Coursework Analyzer** | Upload university PDFs or images and AI converts them into structured portfolio project entries |
| **AI News Feed** | Fetches Reddit tech news, scores each article for relevance to your profile, and surfaces the top 9 |
| **Skill Builder** | Structured multi-path learning system with project roadmaps, step tracking, and daily streak |
| **Admin CMS** | Password-protected content management system for managing every piece of portfolio content |

---

## 3. System Architecture Overview

The platform is built on **Next.js 14 with the App Router**. This means the same codebase handles both the frontend (React components) and the backend (API routes on Node.js). There is no separate backend server.

```
Browser (React / Tailwind CSS)
        │
        │  HTTP requests
        ▼
Next.js 14 App Router (Node.js runtime)
        │
        ├── /app/page.tsx              ← Public portfolio homepage
        ├── /app/admin/page.tsx        ← Admin CMS (password-protected)
        ├── /app/skill-builder/page.tsx ← Skill Builder app
        ├── /app/newsletter/page.tsx   ← AI News Feed
        │
        └── /app/api/                  ← All backend API routes
                ├── resume/tailor      ← 6-step AI pipeline
                ├── resume/compile     ← LaTeX → PDF
                ├── chat               ← Streaming AI chat
                ├── jobs/              ← Job tracker + analysis
                ├── portfolio/         ← CMS read/write
                ├── projects/          ← Project enhancement
                ├── coursework/        ← Document analysis
                ├── news/              ← Reddit + AI curation
                └── upload/            ← File handling
                        │
                        ▼
              src/lib/ai/anthropic.ts  ← Centralized AI client
                        │
                        ▼
              Anthropic Claude claude-sonnet-4-5 API
```

**Key architectural decision:** The Anthropic API key lives only on the server. The browser never touches it. All AI calls go through Next.js API routes, which act as a secure proxy. This is enforced at the code level — a `isStaticDeployment()` check blocks all AI features if the app is running as a static export (e.g., on GitHub Pages) where there is no server to proxy through.

---

## 5. The AI Resume Tailoring Engine — Deep Dive

This is the most technically complex part of the platform. It runs when you call `POST /api/resume/tailor` and has a maximum duration of **180 seconds** because it makes up to 6 sequential Claude API calls plus two LaTeX compilations.

### The Data Merge — Before Any AI Runs

Before the pipeline starts, `ResumeService.loadResumeData()` builds the complete candidate data object by merging two JSON files:

```
portfolio.json                    resume_data.json
    │                                   │
    ├── skills (grouped by category)    ├── personal_info (name, email, phone)
    ├── projects (with technicalDetails)├── education
    ├── experience (flattened roles)    └── pre-written bullets (with real metrics)
    ├── certifications                          │
    └── leadership                             merged into projects & experience
                                               by title/company keyword matching
```

The result is a `ResumeData` object where every project and every job has:
- The full `description` paragraph
- The `technicalDetails` narrative (the granular how-it-was-built detail)
- The `key_features` list
- The `tags` array (skills used)
- `pre_written_bullets` from `resume_data.json` (bullets with real metrics that the AI references)

This merged object is what the AI receives as its "source of truth" for every bullet it writes.

---

### Step 0 — Company & Role Research (`researchCompanyAndRole`)

**Input:** Company name, role name, full job description text  
**Output:** `CompanyResearch` JSON object  
**Fatal if fails:** No — pipeline continues without research

This step asks Claude to build a detailed intelligence profile about the company and role. The prompt positions Claude as a professional career researcher and asks it to synthesize its training knowledge about the company with specific signals from the job description.

The output is a structured JSON with these fields:

```typescript
interface CompanyResearch {
  company_name: string;
  industry: string;
  company_overview: string;          // 2–3 sentence market position summary
  mission_and_values: string[];      // Core company values
  culture_signals: string[];         // JD phrases that signal culture
  role_context: string;              // What this role typically does and why it matters
  ideal_candidate_profile: string;   // Background, mindset, work style they want
  language_to_mirror: string[];      // JD phrases — understood by impression, not copied verbatim
  accomplishments_to_emphasize: string[]; // What types of achievements resonate most
  keywords_to_include: string[];     // Critical ATS filter keywords
  tone: string;                      // formal | professional | startup | technical | business
}
```

The `language_to_mirror` field is the most important one. The prompt explicitly instructs Claude: *do not copy these phrases verbatim into bullets — instead, understand what quality or value each phrase signals, then demonstrate that quality through the candidate's own authentic experience.* This is what makes the tailoring feel natural rather than a copy-paste of JD language.

---

### Step 1 — Job Description Analysis (`analyzeJobDescription`)

**Input:** Full job description text  
**Output:** `JobAnalysis` JSON object  
**Fatal if fails:** Yes — pipeline aborts

This step does structured extraction from the JD:

```typescript
interface JobAnalysis {
  technical_skills: string[];   // e.g. ["Python", "SQL", "Tableau"]
  soft_skills: string[];        // e.g. ["communication", "cross-functional collaboration"]
  experience_areas: string[];   // e.g. ["data analysis", "cloud", "consulting"]
  ats_keywords: string[];       // Critical filter terms
  primary_focus: string;        // 1 sentence: what this role is fundamentally about
  seniority_level: string;      // entry | mid | senior
}
```

This output feeds into every subsequent step. The ATS keywords are checked in the verdict step; the primary focus shapes bullet selection in the tailoring step; the seniority level enforces language rules.

---

### Step 2 — Content Tailoring (`tailorContent`)

**Input:** `JobAnalysis` + `CompanyResearch` + full merged `ResumeData`  
**Output:** `TailoredContent` JSON object  
**Fatal if fails:** Yes

This is the largest and most complex prompt in the system. The full prompt sent to Claude is approximately 3,000 tokens before any candidate data is attached. It contains:

1. **Company & Role Intelligence block** — the full `CompanyResearch` output formatted as clear sections with explicit instructions on how to use each field
2. **Job Analysis block** — the `JobAnalysis` output
3. **Candidate Resume Data block** — the full merged `ResumeData` object as JSON (this is the largest section, often 2,000+ tokens)
4. **Data structure guide** — explains what `technicalDetails`, `pre_written_bullets`, and `tags` mean and how to use each one
5. **Resume template structure** — shows the exact LaTeX layout the AI is generating for, so it understands the visual constraints
6. **Hard budget rules:**
   - Maximum 3 projects selected
   - Maximum 2 work experience entries selected
   - Maximum 3 bullets per included entry
   - Maximum 12 total bullets across all entries
   - Maximum 18 words per bullet
7. **Entry-level language rules** — lists forbidden senior vocabulary (`spearheaded`, `orchestrated`, `architected`, `drove strategic vision`, etc.) and provides correct alternatives
8. **Skill validation rules** — selected skills must be demonstrated in at least one bullet of an entry that has that skill in its `tags`

The output `TailoredContent` contains:

```typescript
interface TailoredContent {
  tailored_professional_summary: string;  // Used for cover letter only (not on resume)
  selected_skills: Record<string, string[]>; // {category: [skill, skill]}
  selected_certifications: Array<{ name: string; year: string }>;
  selected_projects: Array<{
    id: string;
    title: string;
    role?: string;        // Optional subtitle (e.g. "Product Engineer")
    date: string;
    bullets: Array<{
      original_id: string;
      tailored_text: string;
      relevance_score: number;  // 0–100
    }>;
    include: boolean;
  }>;
  selected_work_experience: Array<{
    id: string;
    company: string;
    location: string;
    title: string;
    date: string;
    bullets: Array<{
      original_id: string;
      tailored_text: string;
      relevance_score: number;
    }>;
    include: boolean;
  }>;
  section_order: ['projects', 'work_experience'] | ['work_experience', 'projects'];
  tailoring_summary: string;  // AI's explanation of its decisions
}
```

The `section_order` field is a critical UX decision the AI makes: tech/engineering/data/product roles get `["projects", "work_experience"]` (projects first); business/consulting/sales/ops roles get `["work_experience", "projects"]` (experience first). This single field changes the entire structure of the output PDF.

---

### Step 2.5 — Coherence Pass (`ensureCoherence`)

**Input:** `TailoredContent` draft + `JobAnalysis` + `CompanyResearch`  
**Output:** Refined `TailoredContent` + `CoherenceReport`  
**Fatal if fails:** No — continues with unrefined draft

After the initial tailoring, this pass evaluates whether the bullets work together as a cohesive story, not just as individually optimized points. It checks four things:

1. **Within-entry story** — Do the bullets in each project/job form a logical progression? (e.g., "Built X → Tested Y → Deployed Z" reads better than three disconnected facts)
2. **Cross-entry redundancy** — If Project A and Experience B both say "Used Python for data analysis," one gets reframed to show a different dimension (e.g., "Built Python pipelines to process 10K+ records")
3. **Cross-entry theme reinforcement** — Key JD themes should appear across multiple entries, not buried in one place. If "collaboration" is important, multiple bullets across different entries should demonstrate it
4. **Skill-to-bullet linkage** — Every skill in `selected_skills` must be visibly demonstrated in at least one bullet. If a skill cannot be demonstrated, it gets removed from `selected_skills`

The `CoherenceReport` documents:
```typescript
interface CoherenceReport {
  redundancies_resolved: string[];   // What duplicate bullets were reframed
  stories_built: string[];           // Which entries got logical reordering
  themes_reinforced: string[];       // Which JD themes now appear in multiple places
  summary: string;
}
```

---

### Step 3 — Compatibility Verdict (`runCompatibilityVerdict`)

**Input:** Refined `TailoredContent` + `JobAnalysis` + `CompanyResearch`  
**Output:** `TailoringVerdict`  
**Fatal if fails:** No — skips refinement, goes straight to LaTeX

This is the quality gate. Claude acts as a "senior resume QA specialist" reviewing the draft:

```typescript
interface TailoringVerdict {
  compatibility_score: number;          // 0–100
  compatibility_label: 'Excellent' | 'Strong' | 'Good' | 'Needs Work' | 'Poor';
  needs_refinement: boolean;            // true if score < 80 OR any critical gap
  strengths: string[];                  // What to keep — do not change these
  critical_gaps: Array<{
    area: 'skills' | 'projects' | 'experience' | 'bullets' | 'tone' | 'keywords';
    issue: string;         // Specific problem cited (names actual bullets/entries)
    suggestion: string;    // Exactly what to change
    priority: 'critical' | 'important' | 'minor';
  }>;
  refinement_instructions: string[];    // Directive sentences for the refiner
  summary: string;
}
```

**Scoring scale:**
- 90–100: Excellent — hire immediately, no changes needed
- 75–89: Strong — minor tweaks would help
- 60–74: Good — 1–2 meaningful gaps
- 45–59: Needs Work — notable gaps in coverage or phrasing
- 0–44: Poor — does not adequately address the role

`needs_refinement` is `true` if the score is below 80 OR if any gap has `priority: "critical"`. This triggers Step 4.

---

### Step 4 — Targeted Refinement (`refineTailoredContent`)

**Input:** `TailoredContent` + `TailoringVerdict` + `JobAnalysis` + `CompanyResearch`  
**Output:** Refined `TailoredContent`  
**Fatal if fails:** No — uses pre-refinement draft  
**Only runs if:** `verdict.needs_refinement === true`

This is a surgical fix pass, not a re-run of the full tailoring. The prompt explicitly says: *"Fix ONLY what the verdict flagged. Leave everything else unchanged."*

Hard constraints that cannot be violated in this step:
- No 4th bullet added to any entry (bullet count per entry must not increase)
- No `include: false` entry changed to `include: true` (no new entries added)
- No skills added that aren't already in the portfolio
- Entry-level language enforced
- ≤18 words per bullet
- Same `section_order`, same selected entries, same structure

The `refinement_instructions` from the verdict (e.g., "Rewrite the second Apple bullet to mention data analysis instead of general support") are passed directly into this prompt as a numbered list.

---

### Step 5 — LaTeX Generation (`generateLatexResume`)

**Zero AI calls. Fully deterministic.**

This TypeScript function converts the final `TailoredContent` JSON into a LaTeX document string. It runs in milliseconds.

**Template preamble:** The function reads from `src/templates/resume_template.tex` up to and including `\begin{document}`. This means any edits the admin makes to the template (font size, margins, spacing, new packages) are automatically reflected in every AI-generated resume without touching any code. A hardcoded fallback preamble exists if the file cannot be read.

**Section ordering:** The `section_order` field from the tailored content drives which section appears first:
```typescript
for (const section of sectionOrder) {
  latex += section === 'projects' ? renderProjectWork() : renderWorkExperience();
}
```

**Project entry format:**
```latex
\textbf{Project Title} \hfill Date \\
\textit{Role}          % Only if a role field exists
\begin{itemize}
  \item Bullet one
  \item Bullet two
\end{itemize}
```

**Work experience format:**
```latex
\textbf{Company Name} \hfill Location \\
\textit{Job Title} \hfill Date
\begin{itemize}
  \item Bullet one
\end{itemize}
```

**Fixed layout (always in this order):**
1. Header (name, email, phone, GitHub, LinkedIn)
2. Skills (one `\item` per category: `\textbf{Category:} skill1, skill2`)
3. Section 1 (controlled by `section_order[0]`)
4. Section 2 (controlled by `section_order[1]`)
5. Certifications (all on one line: `Name (Year) | Name (Year)`)
6. Education (always last)

**LaTeX escaping:** Every user-facing string passes through `escapeLatex()` which handles `& % $ # _ { } ~ ^` and backslashes:
```typescript
const replacements: Record<string, string> = {
  '&': '\\&',   '%': '\\%',   '$': '\\$',
  '#': '\\#',   '_': '\\_',   '{': '\\{',
  '}': '\\}',   '~': '\\textasciitilde{}',
  '^': '\\textasciicircum{}',
};
```

---

### Step 6 — Cover Letter Generation

**AI call + deterministic LaTeX wrapper**

`generateCoverLetter()` sends the following to Claude:
- Today's date (formatted as "March 16, 2026")
- Full `CompanyResearch` intel
- First 1,200 characters of the job description
- Tailored professional summary
- Top skills (formatted as "Category: skill1, skill2 | Category: skill3")
- Top 2 bullets from each included project and experience entry

The prompt enforces a strict structure:
1. Date (today's full date)
2. Contact block (Hiring Manager name/title/company/address)
3. Salutation (`Dear Hiring Manager:` or name if found in JD)
4. Opening paragraph (state position, three reasons you are a fit)
5. Middle paragraph(s) (why this company specifically, one or two real accomplishments with specifics)
6. Closing paragraph (restate enthusiasm, thank reader, request discussion)
7. Sign-off (`Sincerely,` + candidate name)

`generateLatexCoverLetter()` wraps the letter body in a LaTeX document with IBM Plex Sans font, `parskip` paragraph spacing (no indents, 10pt between paragraphs), and a matching candidate header.

---

### Pipeline Output

Both the resume and cover letter `.tex` files are saved to the `generated/` directory with a timestamp-based filename. Then both are compiled via `xelatex`. A JSON sidecar file is also saved containing the complete pipeline metadata:

```json
{
  "company_research": { ... },
  "job_analysis": { ... },
  "coherence_report": { ... },
  "tailoring_verdict": { ... },
  "refinement_applied": true,
  "tailored_content": { ... },
  "timestamp": "2026-03-16T..."
}
```

This sidecar is what powers the Job Tracker's stored analysis display in the admin dashboard.

---

## 6. The Centralized AI Client

**File:** `src/lib/ai/anthropic.ts`

This is the single source of truth for all AI operations. Every service and API route calls one of three functions from this file. No AI calls are made anywhere else in the codebase.

### `generate()` — Standard Non-Streaming Call

```
Attempt 1
    │── success → return { text, model }
    │── auth error → throw AIAuthError (no retry)
    │── quota error → throw AIQuotaError (no retry)
    │── retryable error → wait, try again
    │
Attempt 2 (after delay: 3s * 2^0 ± 500ms jitter = ~3–3.5s)
Attempt 3 (after delay: 3s * 2^1 ± 500ms = ~5.5–6.5s)
Attempt 4 (after delay: 3s * 2^2 ± 500ms = ~11.5–12.5s)
Attempt 5 (after delay: 3s * 2^3 ± 500ms = ~23.5–24.5s)
Attempt 6 (after delay: capped at 60s)
    │── all failed → throw AIModelError
```

Per-request timeout is 120 seconds, implemented as a `Promise.race()` between the API call and a `setTimeout` reject.

**JSON mode:** When `jsonMode: true` is passed, the prompt is augmented with: `"IMPORTANT: Return ONLY valid JSON. No markdown, no explanation, no code fences."` The response then passes through `cleanJsonResponse()` and `JSON.parse()`.

**Multimodal support:** Images are passed as `inlineParts` in Gemini-style format (`{ inlineData: { data: base64, mimeType } }`) and auto-converted to Anthropic's content block format before the API call.

### `generateStream()` — Streaming for Chat

This is an `async function*` (async generator). It uses Anthropic's `messages.stream()` API and yields each `text_delta` chunk as it arrives. The chat API route converts this into a `ReadableStream` that the browser reads in real time.

```typescript
const readableStream = new ReadableStream({
  async start(controller) {
    const gen = generateStream(promptText, { ... });
    for await (const chunk of gen) {
      controller.enqueue(encoder.encode(chunk));
    }
    controller.close();
  }
});
```

The browser receives text tokens as they stream in, which is what makes the chat feel instant.

### Error Classification

```typescript
isAuthError():    HTTP 401/403 or "authentication"/"api key" in message → AIAuthError
isQuotaError():   HTTP 429 or "rate limit"/"quota"/"credit balance"     → AIQuotaError
isRetryableError(): HTTP 5xx/529/503/502/500 or "overloaded"/"fetch failed"/"network" → retry
```

---

## 7. The LaTeX PDF Compilation System

**File:** `src/lib/utils/latex-utils.ts`

The `compileLatex()` function shells out to the system `xelatex` binary:

```bash
cd "/path/to/generated" && xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode -output-directory="." "resume.tex"
```

**Critical edge case:** XeLaTeX exits with code **1** for warnings (overfull hbox, missing font glyphs, etc.) even when the PDF is successfully written. If `execSync` throws because of exit code 1, the function checks whether the PDF file actually exists before declaring failure. This prevents a false error on every successful compilation that happens to have a minor warning.

```typescript
} catch (execError: any) {
  // Check if PDF was actually written despite non-zero exit
  if (fileExists(pdfPath)) {
    return { success: true, pdfPath, pdfFilename, size };
  }
  return { success: false, error: execError.message };
}
```

After successful compilation, `cleanupLatexArtifacts()` removes the `.aux`, `.log`, and `.out` files to keep the generated directory clean.

---

## 8. Data Architecture

### `portfolio.json` — Admin Source of Truth

Everything the admin manages flows through this file. The `ResumeService` reads it on startup and re-reads it after any admin save.

```json
{
  "profile": { "name", "role", "tagline", "bio", "email", "location", "availability" },
  "skills": [{ "id", "name", "category" }],
  "experience": [{
    "id", "company", "logo", "website", "location",
    "positions": [{ "role", "period", "description", "technicalDetails", "tags" }]
  }],
  "projects": [{
    "id", "title", "description", "technicalDetails", "key_features",
    "tags", "link", "featured", "images", "hasLiveDemo", "attachments"
  }],
  "certifications": [{ "id", "name", "issuer", "year" }],
  "leadership": [{ "id", "organization", "role", "period", "location", "bullets" }],
  "socialLinks": [...],
  "navItems": [...],
  "siteMetadata": { "title", "description" }
}
```

### `resume_data.json` — Personal Info + Metric Bullets

Stores the information that never changes (personal contact info, education) plus pre-written bullet points that contain real, verified metrics. These bullets are merged into the AI's source data by keyword-matching project titles and company names.

### `job-tracker.json` — Application Records

Auto-created on first save. Each record:
```json
{
  "id": "job_1710000000000",
  "savedAt": "2026-03-16T...",
  "company": "Google",
  "role": "Data Analyst",
  "jobDescription": "...",
  "resumeFilename": "tailored_resume_2026_03_16.pdf",
  "coverLetterFilename": "tailored_cover_letter_2026_03_16.pdf",
  "compatibilityScore": 82,
  "analysis": { ... },
  "applicationStatus": "Applied",
  "notes": "...",
  "qaHistory": [...]
}
```

### Data Merge Logic

`ResumeService.loadResumeData()` performs five merge operations:

1. **Skills** — Groups `portfolio.json` skills by `category` field into a `Record<string, string[]>` object
2. **Projects** — Maps every `portfolio.json` project and attaches `pre_written_bullets` from `resume_data.json` by matching title keywords (word length > 4, case-insensitive)
3. **Experience** — Flattens the multi-position experience format. One company with two roles becomes two separate entries, each with its own `description`, `technicalDetails`, `tags`, and matched `pre_written_bullets`
4. **Certifications** — Direct copy from `portfolio.json`
5. **Leadership** — Direct copy from `portfolio.json`, filtered to non-empty bullets

## 10. Streaming Chat Assistant

**Files:** `src/app/api/chat/route.ts`, `src/components/PortfolioChat.tsx`

### Backend (`/api/chat`)

The chat route loads `portfolio.json` fresh on every request (no caching — edits in the admin reflect immediately in chat). It builds a system instruction that includes the full `profile`, `skills`, `experience`, and `projects` from the portfolio.

The route then calls `generateStream()` and pipes each yielded text chunk into a `ReadableStream`:

```typescript
const readableStream = new ReadableStream({
  async start(controller) {
    const gen = generateStream(promptText, { systemInstruction, history, inlineParts });
    for await (const chunk of gen) {
      controller.enqueue(encoder.encode(chunk));
    }
    controller.close();
  }
});

return new Response(readableStream, {
  headers: { 'Content-Type': 'text/plain; charset=utf-8' }
});
```

### Site Navigation Commands

The AI's system instruction includes: *"Append `[[NAVIGATE:section_id]]` when asked to go to a section."* The `PortfolioChat` component intercepts these commands from the streamed response and calls `document.getElementById(section_id).scrollIntoView()` to navigate the user to that part of the page.

### Vision Support

If the user uploads an image, it is read as a data URI (`data:image/jpeg;base64,...`), the base64 data is extracted, and passed as an `inlinePart` to `generateStream()`. Claude can then analyze and describe the image in the chat response.

### Multi-Turn History

Each chat message is stored in React state as `Array<{ role, content }>`. When a new message is sent, the full history is passed to the API route and then to `generateStream()` as `history`, which builds it into the `messages[]` array sent to Claude. This maintains full conversation context across turns.

## 11. Job Tracker & Skill Gap Analyzer

### Job Tracker API (`/api/jobs`)

Full CRUD on `job-tracker.json`:

| Method | Endpoint | Action |
|---|---|---|
| GET | `/api/jobs` | List all applications |
| POST | `/api/jobs` | Save new application |
| PATCH | `/api/jobs` | Update status/notes/score |
| DELETE | `/api/jobs?id=...` | Delete application |

Each saved application can store the complete AI analysis output alongside the job description, resume/cover letter filenames, compatibility score, application status, and a `qaHistory` array for storing interview Q&A pairs.

### Skill Gap Analyzer (`/api/jobs/analyze`)

**Input:** Job description text  
**Output:** `SkillGapAnalysis` JSON

Claude plays the role of a "brutally honest senior technical recruiter with 20+ years of experience." The candidate profile (skills, experience, projects) is loaded from `portfolio.json` and sent alongside the JD.

```typescript
interface SkillGapAnalysis {
  compatibilityScore: number;         // 0–100
  compatibilityLabel: string;         // "Strong Match" | "Good Match" | "Partial Match" | ...
  industry: string;
  roleType: string;
  seniorityLevel: string;
  presentSkills: string[];            // Skills candidate clearly demonstrates
  missingSkills: Array<{
    skill: string;
    importance: 'critical' | 'important' | 'nice-to-have';
    reason: string;                   // Why this gap matters for this specific role
  }>;
  experienceGaps: string[];           // Domain or industry depth gaps
  criticalVerdict: string;            // 2–4 sentence honest assessment
  improvementPlan: string[];          // Specific, actionable steps
  marketSkillsToLearn: string[];      // Broadly in-demand skills for this role type
  summary: string;
}
```

The prompt explicitly says "DO NOT inflate the score" — the AI is instructed to be harsh and realistic.

## 12. Interview Prep Coach

**Endpoint:** `POST /api/jobs/interview-prep`

**Input:** Question, job description, company, role, and the full `SkillGapAnalysis` from the job tracker

The AI uses the skill gap analysis results (what skills the candidate has, what is missing, the critical verdict) to write answers that are honest about gaps while pivoting to relevant experience.

**Question type detection:**
- Behavioral → STAR format (Situation, Task, Action, Result)
- Situational → Ground in real past experience, then apply to hypothetical
- Skills/Technical → Reference specific projects or roles
- Motivation → Connect genuine background to company and role
- Personal → Draw from real life experiences

**Length rules:** 8–14 sentences for behavioral/complex questions, 4–6 for simple ones.

**Language rules:** Entry-level conversational voice. The prompt specifically bans: `spearheaded`, `orchestrated`, `leveraged`, `synergized`, `drove strategic vision`, `owned end-to-end`. It instructs the AI to write like "a thoughtful, grounded student would actually speak in an interview."

## 13. Coursework Analyzer

**Endpoint:** `POST /api/coursework/analyze`  
**Service:** `src/lib/services/coursework-service.ts`

This feature lets you upload university assignments — PDFs, Word documents, or images — and converts them into professional portfolio project entries.

**How it handles different file types:**

```
.txt  → Read as UTF-8 text, prepend filename header, add as text part
.pdf  → Extract text via pdf-parse (up to 50,000 chars), add as text part
.jpg/.jpeg/.png/.webp/.gif → Read as binary, encode as base64, add as image inlinePart
```

Files over 10MB are skipped with a warning. All parts are assembled into a single Claude call with a prompt asking for a `CourseworkAnalysis` JSON:

```typescript
interface CourseworkAnalysis {
  title: string;            // Professional project title
  description: string;      // 2–3 paragraph description (problem → solution → result)
  tags: string[];           // Skills and technologies
  key_features: string[];   // 3–5 standout capabilities
  technicalDetails: string; // Implementation specifics (architecture, libraries, pipeline)
  analysis_summary: string; // "Analyzed X files..."
}
```

The output maps directly to the project schema in `portfolio.json`. From the admin Coursework tab, one click adds it as a new portfolio project.

## 14. AI-Curated News Feed

**Endpoint:** `GET /api/news`  
**Page:** `src/app/newsletter/page.tsx`

### Step 1: Fetch from Reddit

The endpoint maps a `category` query parameter to a subreddit:

```typescript
const SUBREDDITS: Record<string, string> = {
  tech: 'technology',
  ai: 'artificial',
  web: 'webdev',
  business: 'startups',
};
```

It fetches `https://www.reddit.com/r/{subreddit}/hot.json?limit=25` with a custom User-Agent header. Posts are filtered: stickied posts removed, self-posts (text-only) removed, Reddit-hosted domains removed. The remaining posts are cleaned — HTML entities decoded, thumbnail URLs extracted, dates formatted, up to 20 articles selected.

### Step 2: AI Relevance Scoring

The 20 article titles (without content, for speed) are sent to Claude along with the portfolio `profile.role`, `profile.bio`, top 10 skill names, and project titles. Claude assigns a `relevanceScore` (1–10) and a one-sentence `aiInsight` to each article explaining why it matters to this specific person.

### Step 3: Filter and Sort

Articles with `relevanceScore >= 7` are marked as AI-recommended. All scored articles are sorted by relevance score. The top 9 are returned to the client.

If the AI call fails, the endpoint falls back to sorting by Reddit `score` (upvotes) instead.

**Article Reader:** `GET /api/news/read` uses `@mozilla/readability` + `jsdom` to fetch the full article URL, parse the HTML, and extract the clean article text — the same technique used by apps like Pocket and Instapaper.

## 15. Skill Builder

**Page:** `src/app/skill-builder/page.tsx`  
**Data:** `src/data/skill-paths.json` + `src/lib/skill-paths.ts`

### Architecture

The Skill Builder is a fully client-side React application — no API calls, no server dependency. All progress is persisted to `localStorage` under the key `skill-builder-progress-v2`.

### State Structure

```typescript
interface ProgressData {
  selectedPathId: string | null;
  completedLevels: Record<string, string[]>;  // pathId → completed level keys
  currentLevels: Record<string, string | null>; // pathId → current level key
  completedSteps: Record<string, number[]>;     // "pathId-level" → completed step ids
  streak: number;
  lastActiveDate: string | null;
  totalTimeMinutes: number;
  startedPaths: string[];
}
```

### Streak Calculation

```typescript
function calculateStreak(lastDate: string | null, currentStreak: number): number {
  if (!lastDate) return 1;                    // First use
  const diff = daysBetween(today, lastDate);
  if (diff === 0) return currentStreak;       // Already active today
  if (diff === 1) return currentStreak + 1;   // Consecutive day
  return 1;                                   // Streak broken
}
```

### Views

| View | Component | What It Shows |
|---|---|---|
| `paths` | `PathSelector` | Grid of all learning paths with progress bars |
| `roadmap` | `ProjectRoadmap` | Sequential project levels in selected path |
| `guide` | `ProjectGuide` | Step-by-step instructions for current level |
| `dashboard` | `ProgressDashboard` | Global stats across all paths |

### Session Time Tracking

While the user is in the `guide` view, a `setInterval` runs every 60,000ms and increments `totalTimeMinutes` in state (which is immediately persisted to localStorage). This gives an accurate reading of total time spent learning.

### Statistics (useMemo)

All statistics are derived from state using `useMemo` to avoid recalculation on unrelated renders:
- Total paths, started paths
- Total levels vs. completed levels
- Total steps vs. completed steps
- Overall completion percentage
- Streak, total learning time in hours/minutes
